title: Dry Cache
desc: Hidden shelters in the swamp only stay useful if someone keeps them dry.

steps.text:
No one stays in Gloomuck Swamp longer than they have to without planning ahead. That planning takes the form of dry caches: little platforms and tarred boxes hidden above the high-water line where scouts can leave bandages, tinder, cord, and the sort of food that will not melt, rot, or crawl away before evening.

A cache hand leads you to one tucked under a bent cluster of roots on a low rise just dry enough to count. The platform itself is sound. The trouble is the last two days of damp. One storage satchel feels clammy, and the seal on the main crate lid has begun to peel at one corner.

You can spread the fabric satchels and herb rolls to air, or you can reseal the crate lids and tighten the lashings before the next storm reminds everyone why the cache exists.

options.label: Air the satchels, wraps, and herb rolls
Reseal the crate lids and tighten the lashings

steps.text:
You hang the satchels from the driest roots you can find and unroll each bundle just enough to let the trapped damp escape. Bandage cloth goes first, then waxed map sleeves, then the little packets of bitter leaves scouts chew when swamp stink starts turning their stomachs.

For a while the cache platform looks almost domestic, if your definition of domestic includes black water, mosquito clouds, and something large turning over lazily in the mud below. The cache hand checks each item by touch before rolling it back up. "Good," she says. "Nothing worse than reaching shelter and finding out the shelter brought its own problems."

When the last wrap is repacked, the whole stash feels trustworthy again. In a place as wet and unpleasant as Gloomuck, trust in your supplies is very close to comfort.

options.label: Pack the satchels back into the dry corner

steps.text:
The crate lids are sealed with tarred cloth strips pressed hard into the seams and then lashed down over the top to keep curious wind, water, and wildlife from prying. You warm the tar pot over a travel ember stone, smooth new seal along the edges, and tighten each rope until the wood answers with a dull reliable creak.

The cache hand tests the finished lid with both palms. No give. No soft corner. No place for damp to negotiate an opening. She nods and moves on to the side box while you finish the cross lashings. Under the platform, the swamp gurgles with the steady patience of something that knows most structures eventually lose.

That is what makes the finished crate satisfying. It will not outlast the marsh forever, but it will outlast the next hard rain, and probably the next one after that. Out here, those are practical victories worth having.

options.label: Snuff the tar pot and close the platform hatch
